questions.question: A component has @Input() userId. Where is the first safe place to read userId and make an HTTP call?
options: In the constructor — it's the earliest hook available
In ngOnChanges — it fires before ngOnInit
In ngOnInit — @Input values are set before this hook runs
In ngAfterViewInit — the DOM is fully ready at that point

questions.question: What happens if you start an interval() in ngOnInit and never unsubscribe?
options: Angular automatically stops the interval when the component is destroyed
The interval keeps running and the closed-over component reference prevents garbage collection — a memory leak
The interval throws an error once the component is destroyed
The browser stops the interval after 30 seconds as a security measure

questions.question: Which hook fires every time a bound @Input changes, not just once on initialisation?
options: ngOnInit
ngAfterViewInit
ngOnChanges
ngDoCheck

questions.question: What is the advantage of takeUntilDestroyed(destroyRef) over manually implementing ngOnDestroy?
options: takeUntilDestroyed is faster at runtime
It removes the need for a separate Subscription variable and ngOnDestroy, keeping cleanup declarative and co-located with the subscription
ngOnDestroy only works with class-based components; takeUntilDestroyed works with both
takeUntilDestroyed retries failed HTTP requests automatically

questions.question: You need to get the native DOM element for a canvas after Angular renders the view. Which hook and decorator pair is correct?
options: @ViewChild + constructor
@ViewChild({ static: true }) + ngOnInit — for static references
@ContentChild + ngOnChanges
@ViewChild + ngAfterViewInit — view children exist only after this hook
